children.description: Delete the operational IAM user. Default mode opens with a Pulumi-residue check and refuses if any AWS substrate stack (`aws-eks`, `aws-eks-subzone`, `aws-test`, `aws-ses`) still owns live resources. `--destroy-pulumi-residue` orchestrates `prodbox aws stack <stack> destroy --yes` (the `<stack>` CLI verb is `eks`/`aws-subzone`/`test`/`aws-ses`, not the registry name) in canonical order before deleting the operational IAM user; destroying the long-lived `aws-ses` stack triggers a 5-30 min SES re-verification and ~24h S3 bucket-name cooldown. `--allow-pulumi-residue` is the operator-acknowledged recovery escape hatch that bypasses the residue check entirely (stacks become orphaned). `--destroy-pulumi-residue` and `--allow-pulumi-residue` are mutually exclusive at parse time.

examples.exampleDescription: Delete the operational IAM user (refuses if any AWS substrate stack is live).
Destroy live AWS substrate stacks in canonical order, then delete the operational IAM user.

children.name: teardown

options.description: Render the IAM teardown plan without mutating state
Write the rendered plan to a file
Run `prodbox aws stack <stack> destroy --yes` for each live Pulumi-managed AWS stack in canonical order before deleting the operational IAM user (mutually exclusive with --allow-pulumi-residue)

Bypass the Pulumi-residue refuse-path that prevents deleting the operational IAM user while AWS substrate stacks still have live resources (operator-acknowledged recovery only; mutually exclusive with --destroy-pulumi-residue)

Write the rendered plan to a file
Also reconcile the AWS-gated public edge (Route 53 DNS + ZeroSSL TLS); requires operational aws.*

children.description: Delete the local cluster. Default mode is a PURE LOCAL UNINSTALL: it uninstalls RKE2 and preserves `.data/` (including the MinIO-backed per-run Pulumi state and the durable Vault PV) without querying, gating on, or destroying the per-run AWS Pulumi backend — so per-run AWS stacks (if any) are left untouched and remain destroyable afterward via `prodbox cluster delete --cascade` or `prodbox aws stack <name> destroy --yes`. `--cascade` orchestrates the full teardown (K8s drain + per-run Pulumi destroys + cluster uninstall + postflight tag sweep) as one atomic operator action; the K8s drain phase skips gracefully when no cluster is reachable.

examples.exampleDescription: Uninstall the local cluster (pure local uninstall; leaves per-run AWS stacks untouched).
Orchestrate the full teardown including per-run AWS destroys.

children.name: delete

options.description: Confirm full cluster deletion
Orchestrate the full teardown (K8s drain + per-run Pulumi destroys + cluster uninstall + postflight tag sweep) as one atomic operator action

Write the rendered plan to a file
